dialogue.utterance: Unit 3, dispatch for an adult female with a seizure at the bus stop on Broad Street.
Hi, I'm Maya, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
She had a seizure, full body shaking. It lasted under a minute, that's all we saw.
Ma'am, can you tell me your name?
My name's Lucy, June 16th birthday.
Scored GCS 14. Eyes, verbal, and motor responses are all there. A&O x2.
Airway is patent. No signs of trauma to the head.
Equal chest expansion. Clear lung sounds bilaterally.
Blood pressure is 149 over 89, pulse is 102, and oxygen saturation is 100% on room air.
Respirations are 18, regular and unlabored.
Pupils are equal and reactive. No trauma to the head or neck.
No nuchal rigidity, no signs of jugular venous distention, and no Battle signs.
No epistaxis, no signs of a recent head injury.
We're going to gently press here-any discomfort?
No pain there, no soreness.
No tenderness or abnormalities in the abdomen or pelvic region noted.
Are your arms and legs causing you any discomfort?
Just a small scrape on the right elbow, nothing else.
Right elbow has a 0.5 cm abrasion. Upper extremities show bumps, some open sores, but skin is otherwise normal.
Remainder of skin is unremarkable. No rashes or signs of trauma.
What made the seizure happen? Any medical conditions to consider?
She has epilepsy. She says she took Levetiracetam as usual.
Apply heart monitor now. You'll feel a few sticky patches, nothing more.
EKG shows sinus tachycardia. No acute changes observed.
Some possible signs of anterior infarct, possibly old. Not acute.
Let's start an IV in the left arm. We'll use a 20-Gauge needle for access.
First IV attempt unsuccessful. Vein is blown in the left AC.
Glucose is 141, normal. No issues with low blood sugar here.
We're initiating the seizure protocol based on history and altered mental status.
First 12-lead had too much movement artifact. Let's redo it when you're settled.
Second 12-lead done. Shows right atrial enlargement and occasional PVCs.
Also noted lateral ischemic T waves. Nothing immediately life-threatening but still concerning.
Glucose is now 0. Pulse ox is down to 52%-that's a big drop.
You were lying on your side with a seizure when we arrived. RPD assisted us in lifting you.
Going to VCU Health for further evaluation and monitoring.
Positioned you in semi-Fowler's and secured with straps for transport.
Hospital is aware and expecting you, report given over the phone before arrival.
Bed 30 is ready for you in the ER.
You'll be moved directly into a hospital bed using a draw sheet.
Report given verbally to the ED nurse and doctor. Doctor has signed care transfer.
Eased off the ambulance and smoothly onto the hospital stretcher.
Moved into ED bed 30 using the stretcher with a draw sheet for extra support.
Blood pressure is 142 over 93. Pulse is 103. Breathing remains 18, steady.
Capnography has been drawn, to help confirm airway and respiratory status.
You have no known allergies. You were A&O x2 and gave her name, birthday, and social.
Eyes were open spontaneously, and looking around the environment.
Answered questions with coherent responses regarding personal information.
Moved appropriately when prompted, indicating normal function.
Last oral intake unknown. She didn't report any food or drink recently.
She was on the ground with her hand beneath her head when we arrived.
Skin is warm, dry, and has normal turgor. No rashes or bites observed.
No obstruction in the throat, no swelling or drainage noted in ears or nose.
No signs of neck rigidity or pain. No lumps, masses, or lymphadenopathy.
Alert and oriented to person, place, and time-allegedly A&O x2.
No deformities or bruising on the chest. Breath sounds remain normal.
EKG trending toward atrial enlargement and occasional ventricular issues.
No bowel sounds audible with stethoscope, no palpable masses or distention.
No evidence of discharge, bleeding, or unusual color in urine.
No deformity or angulation in the right elbow, just a minor abrasion.
Seen bumps and sores on upper extremities, likely non-acute from appearance.
Pulse remains consistent, regular in rhythm and quality.
Regular respiratory rate. No flaring, use of accessory muscles observed.
EKG repeatedly showing tachycardia without new acute changes.
We did a second 12-lead EKG now that the patient is more still.
Both upper and lower extremities show normal motor and sensory response.
No neurological deficits noted in the back or spine.
No signs of vertebral column or flank abnormalities.
Pain assessment continues at 0. No localized or generalized pain reported.
You remain secured during the transport with no movement.
Full report over the phone provided during transfer.
Full motion is now under hospital control. No additional movement during transport.
Oxygen saturation remains low while we're in transit.
Glucose continues to show readings at zero, possibly equipment error.
Airway is patent. No signs of respiratory distress.
No anomalies in the cardiac status observed during extended monitoring.
No need for CPAP or oxygen supplementation at this time.
Final location is coordinated, paramedics ready for handover at hospital.
All vital signs and clinical information conformed to doctor's observation upon arrival.
Patient reports no known insurance, must be followed up upon arrival.
Confirmed no trauma, pain, or any episodes since the initial seizure.
Doctor performed final handover and signed clearance to transfer.
You now under the care of the hospital. Our job is complete here.
Could not provide date of last meal or drink. No known food or liquid intake for today.
Could follow simple commands, indicating good gross motor function.
No changes in skin temperature. Peripheral pulses continue to be intact.
She spoke coherently, identified self, date of birth, and was oriented to surroundings.
Eyes were open and tracking well. No signs of fixed gaze or involuntary movement.
Chief complaint remains seizure. No subsequent episodes observed.
Skin on trunk is intact with no signs of burns, bruises, or abrasions.
No signs of edema, cyanosis, or decreased perfusion in trunk or extremities.
Good movement in all extremities. No contractures or deformities noted.
Still reports 0 on numeric pain scale. No signs of distress or discomfort.
Pulse consistent, no significant change from previous readings.
Given the patient's stable condition, ambulance was able to secure for transport.
VCU Health is appropriate facility based on medical needs and condition.
No signs of anaphylaxis or potential airway compromise.
Provided nurse with full complement of vitals, interventions, and history.
Physician accepts report and confirms readiness to manage patient on arrival.
No conflicting or contradicting findings between field care and hospital report.
Arrival confirmed, patient placed in care, and all EMS duties transferred.
